panel.pairing: Hola, ¿cómo estás? — Hello, how are you?
panel.mixology: An interjection — no gender or conjugation. The 'h' is always silent in Spanish.
panel.examples: The all-purpose greeting, fine at any time of day and in any setting, formal or casual.

panel.pairing: Buenos días, señora. — Good morning, ma'am.
panel.mixology: Literally 'good days' — plural by tradition. Note the masculine plural ending '-os'.
panel.examples: Used until around noon. Spanish often greets with the plural form ('días', 'tardes'), which surprises beginners.

panel.pairing: Buenas tardes a todos. — Good afternoon, everyone.
panel.mixology: Feminine plural ('-as') because 'tardes' is feminine — note it differs from 'buenos días'.
panel.examples: Used from about noon until sunset. The shift from 'días' (masc.) to 'tardes' (fem.) trips up many learners.

panel.pairing: Buenas noches, hasta mañana. — Good night, see you tomorrow.
panel.mixology: Feminine plural. Serves as both a greeting (good evening) and a farewell (good night).
panel.examples: Unlike English, this one word covers both arriving in the evening and leaving for bed.

panel.pairing: Adiós, nos vemos. — Goodbye, see you.
panel.mixology: An interjection. Originally 'a Dios' — 'to God' — a blessing for the parting traveler.
panel.examples: Slightly more final than 'hasta luego'; for a quick 'see you soon', Spanish speakers often prefer the latter.

panel.pairing: Hasta luego, amigo. — See you later, friend.
panel.mixology: 'Hasta' = until; 'luego' = later/then. Literally 'until later'.
panel.examples: The everyday casual goodbye, far more common than 'adiós' for ordinary partings.

panel.pairing: ¿Cómo estás hoy? — How are you today?
panel.mixology: Uses 'estar' (temporary states), not 'ser'. Informal 'tú' form; the formal version is '¿Cómo está?'.
panel.examples: Note the opening upside-down question mark '¿' — Spanish brackets every question this way.

panel.pairing: Estoy muy bien, gracias. — I'm very well, thanks.
panel.mixology: 'Muy' (very) + 'bien' (well). 'Muy' modifies adjectives and adverbs and never changes form.
panel.examples: The standard upbeat reply to '¿cómo estás?'. 'Bien' alone ('fine') is also perfectly natural.

panel.pairing: Mucho gusto, soy Ana. — Nice to meet you, I'm Ana.
panel.mixology: Literally 'much pleasure'. A fixed phrase — don't translate it word for word.
panel.examples: Said when meeting someone for the first time. A common reply is 'igualmente' (likewise).

panel.pairing: ¿Cómo te llamas? — Me llamo Juan. — What's your name? — My name is Juan.
panel.mixology: Literally 'how do you call yourself?' — uses the reflexive verb 'llamarse'. The double 'll' sounds like English 'y'.
panel.examples: Answer with 'Me llamo…' (I call myself…). The formal version is '¿Cómo se llama?'.

panel.pairing: Sí, por favor. — Yes, please.
panel.mixology: Note the accent: 'sí' (yes) vs 'si' (if) — the accent mark changes the meaning entirely.
panel.examples: One of the first words to master, and a clear example of how a Spanish accent mark can flip meaning.

panel.pairing: No, gracias. — No, thank you.
panel.mixology: Also the all-purpose negator: place it before a verb to make it negative ('no hablo' = I don't speak).
panel.examples: Spanish doesn't need a helper verb like English 'don't' — just put 'no' in front of the verb.

panel.pairing: Un café, por favor. — A coffee, please.
panel.mixology: Literally 'for favor / as a favor'. A fixed phrase, unchanging.
panel.examples: Politeness essential. Comes at the start or end of a request, just like English 'please'.

panel.pairing: Muchas gracias. — Thank you very much.
panel.mixology: Always plural in form. Strengthen it with 'muchas gracias' (many thanks).
panel.examples: The single most useful courtesy word. 'Muchísimas gracias' is an even warmer 'thanks so much'.

panel.pairing: — Gracias. — De nada. — Thanks. — You're welcome.
panel.mixology: Literally 'of nothing' — equivalent to 'it's nothing'. A fixed reply.
panel.examples: The standard response to 'gracias'. In some regions you'll also hear 'con gusto' or 'a ti'.

panel.pairing: Perdón, ¿dónde está el baño? — Excuse me, where's the bathroom?
panel.mixology: From 'perdonar' (to pardon). Works both to get attention and to apologize.
panel.examples: Use it to squeeze past someone or to begin a question to a stranger — like English 'excuse me'.

panel.pairing: Lo siento mucho. — I'm very sorry.
panel.mixology: Literally 'I feel it' — from 'sentir' (to feel). Reserved for genuine apology or sympathy.
panel.examples: Deeper than 'perdón': use 'lo siento' for real regret or condolences, 'perdón' for minor bumps.

panel.pairing: Perdón, no entiendo. — Sorry, I don't understand.
panel.mixology: From 'entender' (to understand), an irregular stem-changing verb (e→ie).
panel.examples: A survival phrase. Pair it with '¿puede repetir?' (can you repeat?) to keep a conversation going.

panel.pairing: ¿Habla inglés? — Do you speak English?
panel.mixology: Formal 'usted' form of 'hablar'. Informal would be '¿Hablas inglés?'.
panel.examples: Note 'inglés' is lowercase — Spanish doesn't capitalize languages or nationalities.

panel.pairing: Uno, dos, tres. — One, two, three.
panel.mixology: Becomes 'un' before a masculine noun and 'una' before a feminine one ('un libro', 'una casa').
panel.examples: The number one agrees with gender, unlike the others. 'Un' also serves as 'a/an'.

panel.pairing: Dos cafés, por favor. — Two coffees, please.
panel.mixology: Invariable — doesn't change for gender. Same form everywhere.
panel.examples: From two onward, Spanish numbers don't change form for gender (except hundreds).

panel.pairing: Son las tres. — It's three o'clock.
panel.mixology: Invariable. Note it has no accent, but 'treinta' (thirty) and others do.
panel.examples: Used for counting and telling time. 'Las tres' = three o'clock.

panel.pairing: Tengo diez euros. — I have ten euros.
panel.mixology: Invariable. The 'z' is pronounced like 's' in Latin America, like 'th' in much of Spain.
panel.examples: A milestone number; from here you build 'once, doce…' up to twenty.

panel.pairing: Cien personas. — A hundred people.
panel.mixology: 'Cien' before a noun, but 'ciento' in compounds ('ciento uno' = 101).
panel.examples: Exactly 100 is 'cien'; 101 onward switches to 'ciento'. A classic beginner gotcha.

panel.pairing: ¿Qué hora es? — Son las dos. — What time is it? — It's two.
panel.mixology: Literally 'what hour is it?'. Answer with 'Es la una' (1:00) or 'Son las…' (other hours).
panel.examples: Note: 'es la una' is singular for one o'clock, but 'son las' (plural) for every other hour.

panel.pairing: Hoy es lunes. — Today is Monday.
panel.mixology: An adverb of time — invariable. Rhymes with English 'boy'.
panel.examples: Pairs with 'ayer' (yesterday) and 'mañana' (tomorrow) — the core time-frame trio.

panel.pairing: Hasta mañana. — See you tomorrow.
panel.mixology: 'Mañana' means both 'tomorrow' and 'morning' — context decides. The 'ñ' sounds like 'ny' in canyon.
panel.examples: 'la mañana' = the morning; 'mañana' alone = tomorrow. One word, two everyday meanings.

panel.pairing: Ahora no, gracias. — Not now, thanks.
panel.mixology: An adverb. 'Ahora mismo' means 'right now', adding urgency.
panel.examples: Don't confuse with 'ahorita' (Latin American 'in a little while'), which can mean anything from now to later.

panel.pairing: Un vaso de agua, por favor. — A glass of water, please.
panel.mixology: Feminine, but takes 'el' in the singular ('el agua') to avoid two a-sounds clashing. Still feminine: 'el agua fría'.
panel.examples: A famous quirk: 'el agua' looks masculine but is feminine, so adjectives stay feminine ('fría', not 'frío').

panel.pairing: Un café con leche. — A coffee with milk.
panel.mixology: Masculine: 'el café'. Also means 'café' (the place) and the color brown.
panel.examples: 'Café con leche' (with milk) and 'café solo' (espresso) are the two you'll order most.

panel.pairing: Pan, por favor. — Bread, please.
panel.mixology: Masculine: 'el pan'. A short, high-frequency word.
panel.examples: A staple at every meal. 'Pan tostado' is toast; a 'panadería' is the bakery.

panel.pairing: La cuenta, por favor. — The check, please.
panel.mixology: Feminine: 'la cuenta'. From 'contar' (to count).
panel.examples: Essential at restaurants — in Spain and Latin America the bill rarely comes until you ask.

panel.pairing: Una cerveza fría. — A cold beer.
panel.mixology: Feminine: 'la cerveza'. The 'v' is pronounced much like a soft 'b'.
panel.examples: 'Una caña' in Spain is a small draft beer; ordering by size and type varies widely by country.

panel.pairing: La comida está deliciosa. — The food is delicious.
panel.mixology: Feminine: 'la comida'. From 'comer' (to eat). In Spain it often specifically means lunch.
panel.examples: Context matters: 'la comida' can mean food in general, a meal, or specifically the midday meal.

panel.pairing: Quiero un café. — I'd like a coffee.
panel.mixology: From 'querer' (to want), an irregular e→ie verb. 'Quiero' = I want.
panel.examples: Softer and more polite: 'quisiera' ('I would like'). 'Quiero' is direct but perfectly acceptable when ordering.

panel.pairing: ¿Me trae la carta? — Could you bring me the menu?
panel.mixology: Feminine: 'la carta'. Note: 'el menú' usually means a fixed-price set meal, not the menu of dishes.
panel.examples: False friend alert: ask for 'la carta' to see all dishes; 'el menú' often gets you the daily set menu.

panel.pairing: ¡Salud! — Cheers!
panel.mixology: Literally 'health'. A noun used as a toast. The 'd' at the end is barely pronounced.
panel.examples: Doubles as 'bless you!' after a sneeze. Toasting, it's the equivalent of 'cheers'.

panel.pairing: ¿Dónde está la estación? — Where is the station?
panel.mixology: 'Dónde' (with accent) for questions; uses 'estar' for location. Plural: '¿Dónde están…?'.
panel.examples: The single most useful travel question. 'Está' (location) uses 'estar', never 'ser'.

panel.pairing: ¿Dónde está el baño? — Where's the bathroom?
panel.mixology: Masculine: 'el baño'. The 'ñ' gives the 'ny' sound. Also means 'bath'.
panel.examples: In Latin America you may also hear 'el servicio' or 'el sanitario'; in public places, 'los aseos' in Spain.

panel.pairing: Gire a la izquierda. — Turn left.
panel.mixology: Feminine noun used with 'a la izquierda' (to the left). One of Spanish's trickier words to pronounce.
panel.examples: Direction-giving essential. Pairs with 'a la derecha' (to the right) and 'todo recto' (straight ahead).

panel.pairing: A la derecha, por favor. — To the right, please.
panel.mixology: Feminine: 'la derecha'. Don't confuse with 'derecho' (straight ahead / law / right as in 'a right').
panel.examples: Tricky pair: 'la derecha' = the right side; 'derecho' (masc.) = straight ahead. One letter, big difference.

panel.pairing: Estoy aquí. — I'm here.
panel.mixology: Adverb of place. 'Aquí' (here) vs 'allí' (there) vs 'ahí' (right there).
panel.examples: Spanish has a three-way here/there system; 'aquí' is closest to the speaker.

panel.pairing: Está allí. — It's over there.
panel.mixology: Adverb of place — farther than 'ahí'. The 'll' sounds like 'y'.
panel.examples: Part of the aquí / ahí / allí trio marking near, mid, and far distance from the speaker.

panel.pairing: La estación de tren. — The train station.
panel.mixology: Feminine: 'la estación'. Words ending in '-ción' are feminine and become '-ciones' in plural.
panel.examples: Reliable rule: nearly all '-ción' words are feminine (la nación, la información) — and they drop the accent in the plural.

panel.pairing: ¿Cuánto cuesta? — How much is it?
panel.mixology: From 'costar' (to cost), an o→ue stem-changing verb. 'Cuánto' takes an accent in questions.
panel.examples: Shopping survival phrase. For multiple items: '¿Cuánto cuestan?' (plural).

panel.pairing: Yo soy estudiante. — I am a student.
panel.mixology: Subject pronoun. Often dropped, since the verb ending already shows 'I' ('soy' = I am).
panel.examples: Spanish frequently omits subject pronouns — 'soy' alone means 'I am'. Use 'yo' for emphasis or contrast.

panel.pairing: ¿Tú hablas español? — Do you speak Spanish?
panel.mixology: Informal singular 'you'. Accent matters: 'tú' (you) vs 'tu' (your).
panel.examples: Use 'tú' with friends, family, peers, and children. For respect or distance, use 'usted'.

panel.pairing: Él es alto. Ella es alta. — He is tall. She is tall.
panel.mixology: 'Él' (he, with accent) vs 'el' (the). 'Ella' = she. Adjectives agree: 'alto' / 'alta'.
panel.examples: Notice the adjective changes ending to match gender — a core habit to build early.

panel.pairing: Nosotros somos amigos. — We are friends.
panel.mixology: Masculine/mixed group: 'nosotros'; all-female group: 'nosotras'.
panel.examples: A mixed-gender group defaults to the masculine form — true of nearly all Spanish plurals.

panel.pairing: Es mi mejor amigo. — He's my best friend.
panel.mixology: 'el amigo' (male friend), 'la amiga' (female friend). Gender follows the person.
panel.examples: Many people-nouns swap '-o'/'-a' for male/female. 'Amigos' can mean an all-male or mixed group of friends.

panel.pairing: Mi familia es grande. — My family is big.
panel.mixology: Feminine: 'la familia'. Singular even though it refers to many people.
panel.examples: A collective noun treated as singular: 'la familia es…', not 'son'.

panel.pairing: Mi madre se llama Rosa. — My mother's name is Rosa.
panel.mixology: Feminine: 'la madre'. Affectionate forms: 'mamá', 'mami'.
panel.examples: 'Madre' is the standard word; 'mamá' is the warmer everyday term, like 'mom'.

panel.pairing: Mi padre trabaja mucho. — My father works a lot.
panel.mixology: Masculine: 'el padre'. Plural 'los padres' means 'parents' (mother and father together).
panel.examples: Useful quirk: 'los padres' = 'the parents', not 'the fathers' — the masculine plural covers both.

panel.pairing: El hombre y la mujer. — The man and the woman.
panel.mixology: 'el hombre' (man, masc.), 'la mujer' (woman, fem.). The 'j' in 'mujer' is a throaty 'h' sound.
panel.examples: Two of the most common nouns, and a clean illustration of natural gender matching grammatical gender.

panel.pairing: Soy de España. — I'm from Spain.
panel.mixology: Irregular. Used for identity, origin, time, and lasting traits. Conjugates: soy, eres, es, somos, son.
panel.examples: Spanish has two verbs for 'to be'. 'Ser' is for who/what something fundamentally is — origin, profession, character.

panel.pairing: Estoy cansado. — I'm tired.
panel.mixology: Irregular. Used for location, moods, and changing states. Conjugates: estoy, estás, está, estamos, están.
panel.examples: The other 'to be'. 'Estar' covers location and temporary conditions. 'Estoy bien' = I'm (feeling) well.

panel.pairing: Tengo dos hermanos. — I have two siblings.
panel.mixology: Irregular (tengo, tienes, tiene…). Also expresses age: 'tengo 20 años' (I'm 20).
panel.examples: Spanish uses 'tener' (to have) for age, hunger, and thirst: 'tengo hambre' literally = 'I have hunger'.

panel.pairing: Voy a casa. — I'm going home.
panel.mixology: Highly irregular (voy, vas, va, vamos, van). 'Ir a' + verb expresses the near future.
panel.examples: 'Voy a comer' = 'I'm going to eat' — the easiest way to talk about the future before learning the future tense.

panel.pairing: Hablo un poco de español. — I speak a little Spanish.
panel.mixology: Regular '-ar' verb — the model for a huge group. Conjugates: hablo, hablas, habla, hablamos, hablan.
panel.examples: Learn 'hablar' and you've learned the pattern for thousands of regular '-ar' verbs.

panel.pairing: ¿Quieres comer algo? — Do you want to eat something?
panel.mixology: Regular '-er' verb — the model for that group. Conjugates: como, comes, come, comemos, comen.
panel.examples: The template for regular '-er' verbs. Note 'como' also means 'how/as' — context separates them.

panel.pairing: Te quiero. — I love you.
panel.mixology: Irregular e→ie (quiero, quieres, quiere…). Means both 'to want' and, with people, 'to love'.
panel.examples: 'Te quiero' is the everyday 'I love you' for family and partners; 'te amo' is more intense and romantic.

panel.pairing: ¿Puedes ayudarme? — Can you help me?
panel.mixology: Irregular o→ue (puedo, puedes, puede…). Followed by another verb in the infinitive.
panel.examples: Essential for polite requests: '¿Puede…?' (formal) and '¿Puedes…?' (informal) both mean 'Can you…?'.

panel.pairing: ¿Qué haces? — What are you doing?
panel.mixology: Irregular (hago, haces, hace…). Covers both 'do' and 'make'. Also used for weather: 'hace calor' (it's hot).
panel.examples: One verb for both 'do' and 'make'. It also drives weather expressions: 'hace frío' = it's cold.

panel.pairing: — Gracias. — No hay problema. — Thanks. — No problem.
panel.mixology: 'Hay' = there is/are (from 'haber'). Literally 'there is no problem'.
panel.examples: 'Hay' is one of the handiest words in Spanish: 'there is' and 'there are' in a single form.

panel.pairing: — ¿Vienes? — ¡Claro! — Are you coming? — Of course!
panel.mixology: An adverb/interjection here. Also an adjective meaning 'clear' or 'light' (color).
panel.examples: 'Claro' or 'claro que sí' is an enthusiastic 'of course!'. Extremely common in conversation.

panel.pairing: — Nos vemos a las dos. — Vale. — See you at two. — OK.
panel.mixology: From 'valer' (to be worth). In Spain it's the all-purpose 'OK'; less common in Latin America.
panel.examples: A regional marker: 'vale' instantly signals Spain. In Latin America you'll hear 'okay', 'bueno', or 'dale'.

panel.pairing: ¿Qué tal? — How's it going?
panel.mixology: Literally 'what such?'. A fixed, casual greeting — no verb needed.
panel.examples: More casual than '¿cómo estás?'. Can also mean 'how about…?' ('¿Qué tal el hotel?' = How's the hotel?).

panel.pairing: Tengo hambre. ¿Comemos? — I'm hungry. Shall we eat?
panel.mixology: Uses 'tener' (to have) + 'hambre' (hunger) — literally 'I have hunger', not 'I am hungry'.
panel.examples: A key difference from English: hunger, thirst, and age use 'tener'. The 'h' in 'hambre' is silent.

panel.pairing: Perdón, estoy perdido. — Sorry, I'm lost.
panel.mixology: Uses 'estar' (temporary state) + 'perdido/a'. Women say 'perdida'.
panel.examples: Adjective agreement in action: a man says 'perdido', a woman 'perdida'. A vital travel phrase.

panel.pairing: Me gusta el café. — I like coffee.
panel.mixology: Literally 'it pleases me' — from 'gustar', which works backwards from English. Plural things: 'me gustan'.
panel.examples: 'Gustar' flips the logic: the thing liked is the subject. 'Me gustan los gatos' = 'cats please me' = I like cats.

panel.pairing: ¡Buen provecho! — Enjoy your meal!
panel.mixology: A fixed phrase, the Spanish equivalent of 'bon appétit'. 'Buen' is the shortened form of 'bueno' before a noun.
panel.examples: Said before eating, or politely to others who are eating as you pass. Universally warm and common.
